---
title: "Statistical analysis plan for calibration of the Trident trial to a population-based
  cohort in Scotland."
author: "Dr Alice Hosking, Prof Rustam Al-Shahi Salman, Prof William Whiteley, Prof David McAllister"
email: "alice.hosking@ed.ac.uk"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  word_document:
    toc: true
  # html_document:
  #   toc: true
    number_sections: true
    # theme: united
bibliography: calibration.bib
csl: "american-medical-association-10th-edition.csl"

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(data.table)
library(flexsurv)
library(survival)
library(SurvRegCensCov)
library(muhaz)
library(ggsurvfit)
library(survminer)
library(rmeta)
library(flextable)

set.seed(42)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(message = F, warning=F, echo=F)

set_flextable_defaults(
  font.size = 10, theme_fun = theme_vanilla,
  padding = 6,
  background.color = "#FFFFFF")
```

## Introduction

### Purpose and scope of this document

This statistical analysis plan details the analyses planned for calibration of the TRIDENT trial to a population-based inception cohort, including a worked-through example with mock values of hazard ratios.

### Study overview

Intracerebral haemorrhage causes around a third of strokes worldwide but half the death and disability after stroke.[@Feigin2024] Those with ICH have an estimated 2% annual risk of recurrent ICH and 2% annual risk of ischaemic stroke.[@Putri2026] A single-pill combination of three blood pressure lowering medicines at half standard dose (telmisartan 20 mg, amlodipine 2.5 mg, and indapamide 1.25 mg) in addition to standard care could reduce the risk of  recurrent stroke after ICH. TRIDENT was an international, double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomised trial in adults with ICH and mild-moderate hypertension (systolic BP: 130–160 mmHg). The primary outcome was any stroke; secondary outcomes include recurrent ICH, cardiovascular events, and safety.[@Chalmers2026]

However, TRIDENT, as with any other trial, might be subject to selection bias, leading to low representativeness of the general population (i.e., lack of external validity).[@Westreich2019; @Bareinboim2016;@Rothwell2005] In particular, women, older people, and those with frailty and multimorbidity, are likely underrepresented in this trial.[@Sheppard2020; @Sardar2014; @Carcel2021] Furthermore, the trial was conducted in countries with different demography to the UK.The mean age of people with incident ICH in two pooled cohorts from Oxfordshire, England and Lothian, Scotland was 74.7 years, with 53% of the cohort being female.@Li2021 TRIDENT only includes those who survived long enough to be enrolled in the trial; nevertheless, the mean age was 58 years in both treatment and placebo arm, and women comprised 33% and 34% of each arm.

In recent years statistical trial calibration (or transportation), has been developed to deal with this issue.[@Cole2010;@Manke-Reimers2025] Calibration refers to the methods used to extend the results of a clinical trial to a different population than the one originally studied. Rather than using observational studies to assess the same clinical question, calibration keeps the randomisation intact and therefore does not introduce confounding bias. This process provides a quantitative estimate of the external validity of trial findings.


### Objectives

The aim of this study is to calibrate the effect of a low-dose triple pill antihypertensive medication in people with ICH from the TRIDENT trial to a population-based cohort in Lothian, Scotland.

The outcome will be:

* A measure of relative effect, the risk ratio, calibrated to the Lothian population.
* A measure of absolute effect, the number needed to treat, calibrated to the Lothian population.


## Study design, population and outcomes

### Study design

This study will use stratified data from the TRIDENT trial (the trial population) and data from the LATCH study (the target population), a prospective, population-based inception cohort study of people with ICH in NHS Lothian. 

The TRIDENT trial result showed no difference in the primary outcome (recurrent stroke) until approximately 18 months, from which point the cumulative incidence function for the primary outcome appeared to diverge between control and treatment arms. We will therefore calibrate at two time points, 18 months ($t_1$) and end of follow up (approx 6 years, $t_2$). 

There are various methods to calibrate trials. Regression-based approaches and inverse probability weighting require individual patient data.[@Wei2023] Alternatively, one can stratify a trial and target population by key factors, and extract hazard ratios from the trial for each stratum, obviating the need for individual participant data access. We propose to use this method to calibrate TRIDENT to the LATCH population. 

We will calibrate based on age and sex, two factors by which the TRIDENT population differs to the UK population with ICH. Age is strongly related to other characteristics such as multimorbidity and frailty which are commonly under-represented in clinical trials, but were not directly measured in TRIDENT.@Barnett2012

In summary, the method is:

* Inspect the distribution of ages in TRIDENT and LATCH to choose stratification ages with sufficient overlap between LATCH and TRIDENT.
* Split TRIDENT into strata of age and sex (eg <65 years female, <65 years male, over 65 years female,  over 65 years male)
* Obtain hazard ratios and their 95% confidence intervals for TRIDENT for each stratum at 18 months and from 18 months to end of trial (assumed to be 6 years for this document). 
* Split LATCH into the same age- and sex-based strata. For each strata, fit a survival model to obtain rates and confidence intervals of the primary outcome at $t_1$ and $t_2$.
* Use these rates, and the hazard ratios from TRIDENT, to model absolute rates in untreated and treated in each stratum.
* The relative risk for each stratum is then (risk untreated/risk treated). We combine relative risks as a weighted mean, with the weights the number of people in each stratum in LATCH.

![Figure 1: Flowchart schematic of trial calibration method using two strata.](/Users/alicehosking/Documents/REA3 clinical fellow job/Treatment effect in trials/calibration-of-trials/Trial calibration flow chart.png){#id .class width=90%}


### Populations

#### Trial population

The trial population is the 1670 people included in the TRIDENT trial, an international multicentre double-blind placebo-controlled trial. The average time to randomisation after ICH onset was 54 days. The primary outcome was time to first recurrent stroke.

TRIDENT Inclusion Criteria

* Adults (≥18 years) with a history of primary ICH that was confirmed by imaging.
* Clinically stable, as judged by investigator.
* An average of two resting systolic blood pressure (SBP) levels measured 5 minutes apart in the range 130-160 mmHg recorded in a seated position. Patients with higher SBP could still be included if it was considered by the site investigator that their management would be consistent with local standards of clinical practice.
* Geographical proximity to the recruiting hospital and/or follow-up medical clinic site to allow ready access for in-person assessments during follow-up.
* No clear contraindication or indication to any of the individual components of the Investigational Medical Product (IMP).
* Provision of written informed consent

TRIDENT Exclusion Criteria

* Taking an angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor (ACE-I) that could not be switched to any of the following alternatives: an angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) (e.g. telmisartan 20 or 40 mg), a calcium channel blocker (CCB) (e.g. amlodipine 2.5 or 5 mg), or a thiazide (TZ)-like diuretic (e.g. indapamide 1.25 mg); an equivalent class (ARB, CCB or TZ); or a beta-blocker (BB).
* Contraindication to any component of the IMP in the context of currently prescribed BP lowering treatment.
* Unable to complete the study procedures and/or follow-up.

#### Target population

We aim to calibrate to a target population that reflects who might be prescribed the TRIDENT single pill, if it were recommended in clinical treatment guidelines. 

The Lothian Audit of Intracerebral Haemorrhage (LATCH) was a population-based cohort study that ran between 1st June 2010 and 31st May 2013, and 1st November 2018 and 31st October 2021, with the aim of near-complete ascertainment of ICH in the Lothian Health board region of Scotland in residents aged ≥16 years. Approval for LATCH was granted by the Caldicott guardian of NHS Lothian. The LATCH audit has been described previously.[@Samarasekera2015] Prospective sources included a trust-wide network of physicians, neurologists, neurosurgeons, radiologists, pathologists, specialist nurses and audit personnel; and daily neuroradiology meetings and review of all computed tomographic (CT) head scans. Retrospective sources were electronic patient notes, International Classification of Diseases 10th Revision (ICD-10) coded hospital discharge records, the Scottish Stroke Care Audit,[@Turner2015] and death records at the Office of the Procurator Fiscal.  

The LATCH cohort includes 1077 people with ICH of any cause; 917 had ICH presumed due to microvascular cause.  We aim to identify those in LATCH who might be prescribed the TRIDENT single pill, if it were recommended in clinical treatment guidelines, based on the TRIDENT inclusion criteria and prescribing practice:

*	In clinical practice, antihypertensive treatment for secondary prevention is often started once a person is clinically stable. In the LATCH cohort, a third of people die within 7 days of ICH; beyond 7 days, the death rate reduces, and we pragmatically assume those alive at 7 days after ICH are clinically stable; there are 602 people alive at 7 days. 
*	We did not have measurement of post-ICH blood pressure for all of LATCH. Almost everyone with intracerebral haemorrhage is hypertensive; we expect very few to have blood pressure . If the triple pill used in TRIDENT were used in clinical practice, there would likely be no upper restriction on pre-treatment blood pressure; therefore we do not limit by blood pressure.
*	The TRIDENT pill would only be used on those able to get out of bed; therefore, we will limit inclusion to those with pre-morbid modified Rankin Scale (mRS) of 4 or less. All 602 who survived 7 days had a pre-morbid mRS of 4 or less.


#### Assumptions and limitations

* We assume that the hazard ratios for each stratum reflect real variation in hazard by age and sex, rather than random variation around the mean.
* The trial and target populations are partially overlapping, as the Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh recruited 17 people with ICH for TRIDENT. Therefore approximately 9 people in the target population (1.5%) are on treatment. We cannot identify those treated, but assume this will make a negligible difference to the untreated rate.
* There were limitations to the maximum doses of additional blood pressure treatment for those in both treatment and placebo arms of TRIDENT. People in LATCH may have been prescribed doses in excess of this, but we do not have data on discharge medications for the whole LATCH population, so cannot exclude these people.


## Analysis example

We now show the method for calibration through a worked example using real LATCH data, and mock hazard ratios (and their 95% confidence intervals) from TRIDENT. We create age/sex four strata for this example.

### Time period definitions

We will calibrate at two time points, 18 months ($t_1$) and end of follow up (approx 6 years, $t_2$). The time period from start of follow-up to $t_1$ is $\tau_1$. The time period from $t_1$ to $t_2$ is $\tau_2$.

### Data tidying

First we filter LATCH to only those who survived 7 days, format dates correctly, set censoring dates, and split into strata (under 65 and 65 and over, male and female). For this study, we censor LATCH follow up at the maximum TRIDENT follow-up time of six years.

```{r read in data, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
latch_for_tridentx<- read_csv("/Users/alicehosking/Documents/REA3 clinical fellow job/Treatment effect in trials/LATCH cohort survived 7 days.csv")
setDT(latch_for_tridentx)
latch_for_trident<- latch_for_tridentx[,c("survival_time","censor_status_death","i_date","Gender_t", "date_of_death.y","cause_of_death","first_mi","first_tia","first_stroke","first_dvt","first_pe", "first_rec_ich","pre_rankin", "age_index")] 
#some dates are stored as excel formatted dates. 
#for this analysis, we care about date of first stroke (which is ischaemic stroke) and date of recurrent ICH
latch_for_trident[, first_rec_ich:= as.Date(first_rec_ich, origin = "1899-12-30")][, first_stroke:= as.Date(first_stroke, origin = "1899-12-30")]

#time to first event
latch_for_trident[, time_to_ich:= difftime(first_rec_ich,i_date,  units="days")][,time_to_isch_str:=difftime(first_stroke ,i_date,  units="days")][, time_to_all_stroke:= pmin(time_to_ich, time_to_isch_str, na.rm=T)][,date_of_death_trid:= as.Date(date_of_death.y, origin = "1899-12-30")] [, time_to_death:= difftime(date_of_death_trid,i_date,  units="days")]

# if no first event, time to end of death/ end study
latch_for_trident[, surv_time_all_stroke:=fifelse(is.na(time_to_all_stroke)==T&is.na(time_to_death)==T, difftime("2022-12-31", i_date, units="days"), fifelse(is.na(time_to_all_stroke)==T&is.na(time_to_death)==F, time_to_death, time_to_all_stroke))]

#censor status and survival time at 1.5 years, 4.5 years and all time
latch_for_trident[, censor_status_all_str_all_time:= fifelse(is.na(time_to_all_stroke)==T,0,1)][,censor_all_str_t1:= fifelse(time_to_all_stroke<548,1,0)][ ,censor_all_str_t1:=fifelse(is.na(time_to_all_stroke)==T,0,censor_all_str_t1)][, survival_stroke_t1:= fifelse(censor_all_str_t1==1, time_to_all_stroke, pmin(surv_time_all_stroke, time_to_death, na.rm = T))][, survival_stroke_t1:= pmin(548,survival_stroke_t1)][,censor_all_str_t2:= fifelse(time_to_all_stroke<6*365.25,1,0)][ ,censor_all_str_t2:=fifelse(is.na(time_to_all_stroke)==T,0,censor_all_str_t2)][, survival_stroke_t2:= fifelse(censor_all_str_t2==1, time_to_all_stroke, pmin(surv_time_all_stroke, time_to_death, na.rm = T))][,survival_stroke_t2:=pmin(round(6*365.25,0),survival_stroke_t2)]

strata<- list(
latch_under_65_m<- latch_for_trident[age_index<65&Gender_t=="Male"],
latch_under_65_f<- latch_for_trident[age_index<65&Gender_t=="Female"],
latch_over_65_m<- latch_for_trident[age_index>64&Gender_t=="Male"],
latch_over_65_f<- latch_for_trident[age_index>64& Gender_t=="Female"]
  
)

strata_n<- c()
strata_n_events_t1<- c()
strata_n_events_t2<- c()
strata_fu_t1 <- c()
strata_fu_t2<- c()

for(i in 1:length(strata)){
  strata_n[i] <-length(strata[[i]]$i_date) 
  strata_n_events_t1[i]<- sum(strata[[i]]$censor_all_str_t1)
  strata_n_events_t2[i]<- sum(strata[[i]]$censor_all_str_t2)
  strata_fu_t1[i] <- sum(strata[[i]]$survival_stroke_t1)
  strata_fu_t2[i]<- sum(strata[[i]]$survival_stroke_t2)

}

```


### Parametric survival model for LATCH {.tabset}

We model the event rate (stroke recurrence) in LATCH for each stratum. We use a parametric model. 

First inspect the Kaplan Meier curve. This looks approximately exponential. 

We model the event rate with a parametric survival model to select the most appropriate distribution, using the flexsurv package in R. There are various choices for the model distribution (exponential, gamma, Weibull etc). We compare the hazard and survival plots for each distribution to a non-parametric model (the kernel, in black), and the AIC for each distribution.

The exponential model allows much simpler calculations other models. The AICs are all similar (gamma is the lowest). Although other models have marginally better fits, the exponential model fit is acceptable, and for analytical simplicity, we choose an exponential model.


#### Kaplan Meier curve


```{r KM for whole population, warnings=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.width=6,fig.height=4}

s1<- survfit(Surv(survival_stroke_t2, censor_all_str_t2) ~ 1, data = latch_for_trident)
km_plot<- survminer::ggsurvplot(s1, ylim=c(0.6, 1), xlim=c(0, 6*365.25), break.x.by = 365.25, xlab = "Year", risk.table =FALSE,surv.plot.height = 2,
           ggtheme = theme(aspect.ratio = 1),legend="none", ylab="Survival free of recurrent stroke") 
km_plot$plot+  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0,365.25,365.25*2, 365.25*3, 365.25*4, 365.25*5, 365.25*6),
                                     labels = c(0,1,2,3,4,5,6))
```



```{r choose parametric model for event rate}

# help here: https://www.r-bloggers.com/2019/06/parametric-survival-modeling/
#from help page - choosing best model based on shape of distribution

# cran vignette from flexsurv package also useful
# https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/flexsurv/vignettes/standsurv.html

#non-parametric model for comparison
kernel_haz_est <- muhaz(as.difftime(latch_for_trident$surv_time_all_stroke, units="days"), latch_for_trident$censor_status_all_str_all_time)
kernel_haz <- data.table(time = kernel_haz_est$est.grid, est = kernel_haz_est$haz.est,method = "Kernel density")

#vectors of all the options
dists <- c("exp", "weibull",  "gamma", "lognormal", "llogis", "gengamma","gompertz")
dists_long <- c("Exponential", "Weibull (AFT)", "Gamma", "Lognormal", "Log-logistic", "Generalized gamma","Gompertz")

#parametric model - generate hazards and survival for different distributions, for whole dataset
#initialise variables
parametric_haz <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(dists))
parametric_preds <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(dists))
fit_test<- list()

#loop through each distribution
for (i in 1:(length(dists)-1)){
  fit <- flexsurvreg(Surv(survival_stroke_t2, censor_all_str_t2) ~ 1, data = latch_for_trident, dist = dists[i]) 
  fit_test[[i]]<- fit
  #hazards for plot 1
  parametric_haz[[i]] <- summary(fit, type = "hazard", ci = FALSE, tidy = TRUE)
  parametric_haz[[i]]$method <- dists_long[i]
  #survival for plot 2
  parametric_preds[[i]] <- summary(fit, type = "survival", tidy=T)
  parametric_preds[[i]]$method <- dists_long[i]
  
  }

#add in gompertz separately as failed to initialize
fit_gompertz <- flexsurvreg(Surv(survival_stroke_t2, censor_all_str_t2) ~ 1, data = latch_for_trident, dist = dists[7], inits=c(-1, 1/mean(as.numeric(latch_for_trident$surv_time_all_stroke)))) 
parametric_haz[[length(dists)]] <- summary(fit_gompertz, type = "hazard", 
                                     ci = FALSE, tidy = TRUE)
parametric_haz[[length(dists)]]$method<- dists_long[length(dists)]
parametric_preds[[length(dists)]] <- summary(fit_gompertz, type = "survival", 
                                     ci = FALSE, tidy = TRUE)
parametric_preds[[length(dists)]]$method<- dists_long[length(dists)]

#join all together
parametric_haz <- rbindlist(parametric_haz)
haz <- rbind(kernel_haz, parametric_haz)
haz[, method := factor(method,
                       levels = c("Kernel density",
                                  dists_long))]
n_dists <- length(dists) 
```
#### Plot of survivals of various distributions

```{r survival plots,fig.width=8,fig.height=4}

parametric_preds_df<- rbindlist(parametric_preds, fill=TRUE)
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(aes(x=time, y=est, col = method, linetype = method ), data=parametric_preds_df) + 
  geom_step(aes(x=time, y=surv), data=km_plot$data.survplot)+
  xlab("Years") +ylab("Survival free of recurrent stroke") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0,365.25,365.25*2, 365.25*3, 365.25*4, 365.25*5, 365.25*6),
                                     labels = c(0,1,2,3,4,5,6))
```

#### Plot of hazards of various distributions
```{r hazard plot,fig.width=8,fig.height=4}

ggplot(haz, aes(x = time, y = est, col = method, linetype = method)) +
  geom_line() +
  xlab("Days") + ylab("Hazard") + 
  scale_colour_manual(name = "", 
                      values = c("black", rainbow(n_dists))) +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = "",
                        values = c(1, rep_len(2:6, n_dists)))+
  xlim(0, 365.25*6)+
  xlab("Years") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0,365.25,365.25*2, 365.25*3, 365.25*4, 365.25*5, 365.25*6),
                                     labels = c(0,1,2,3,4,5,6), limits = c(0,365.35*6))

```



#### Comparison of AIC

```{r aic}

parametric_haz <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(dists))
fit_test<- list()
fit_aic<- c()
for (i in 1:(length(dists)-1)){
  fit <- flexsurvreg(Surv(survival_stroke_t2, censor_all_str_t2) ~ 1, data = latch_for_trident, dist = dists[i]) 
  fit_test[[i]]<- fit
  fit_aic<- c(fit_aic, fit$AIC)
}

#add in gompertz separately
fit_gompertz <- flexsurvreg(Surv(survival_stroke_t2, censor_all_str_t2) ~ 1, data = latch_for_trident, dist = dists[7], inits=c(-1, 1/mean(as.numeric(latch_for_trident$time_to_all_stroke)))) 
fit_aic<-  c(fit_aic, fit_gompertz$AIC)
#join all together
parametric_aic<- as.data.frame(cbind(dists_long, round(fit_aic,2)))
colnames(parametric_aic)<- c("Model", "AIC")
ft<- flextable(parametric_aic)
ft <- width(ft,width=dim_pretty(ft)$widths)
ft

```


### Event rates in LATCH {.tabset}

Assuming exponential event rate across $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$, we can calculate the event rate, the standard error, and simulate a normally distributed sample.@Greenland2004 Throughout, we simulate with 10,000 samples. We plot the sample and show the event rates for each stratum in a table.

The event rate is defined as:

$$\lambda_u=\text{rate in untreated}$$
We define $n_s$ as the number of people in each stratum, and $m_s$ as the number of events per stratum.

$$\lambda_u=\frac{m_s}{\text{Total follow up time for stratum}}$$.

As we are assuming an exponential distribution, we can calculate the standard error directly:

$$ ln(SE)= \frac{1}{\sqrt{m_s}}$$.

We can then simulate a normally distributed sample for the event rates.

```{r simple event rates}
## Set number of samples with nrep for use throughout.
nrep<-10000

#event rate at t1 and t2
rate_est <- strata_n_events_t2/ strata_fu_t2*365.35*100
log_rate_est <- log(strata_n_events_t2/ strata_fu_t2)
# Calculate SE of the log-rate for untreated
log_rate_se <- 1 / sqrt(strata_n_events_t2)
## obtain and plot samples from rate in untreated
untreated_rates<- as.data.frame(cbind(strata_n, rate_est,log_rate_est, log_rate_se))
setDT(untreated_rates)
log_lambda_u<- untreated_rates[,rnorm(nrep, mean=log_rate_est, sd=log_rate_se), by=.I]

#rename I column to the strata names
strata <-c("Male <65 years", "Female <65 years", "Male 65 years and over", "Female 65 years and over")
log_lambda_u$I <- factor(log_lambda_u$I, levels=c(1,2,3,4), labels=strata)

#calculate confidence intervals for visualisation
untreated_rates[, lower_ci:= exp(log_rate_est-1.96*log_rate_se)*365.35*100][, upper_ci:= exp(log_rate_est+1.96*log_rate_se)*365.35*100]

#round all the numbers
untreated_rates<- round(untreated_rates,2)
#make the rate and confidence intervals look nice in one column
untreated_rates[, rate_cis:= paste(rate_est, " (", lower_ci, "-", upper_ci, ")", sep="")][,rate_est:=NULL][, lower_ci:=NULL][, upper_ci:=NULL]

```

#### Table of event rates for each stratum

```{r table of rates}
#print table of rates
untreated_rates<- as.data.frame(untreated_rates)
rownames(untreated_rates)<- strata



colnames(untreated_rates) <- c( "N in stratum",  "log(rate/day)", "log(standard error)","Events per 100 person years (95% CI)")


ft_rates<- flextable(untreated_rates)

ft_rates2 <- width(ft_rates,width=dim_pretty(ft_rates)$widths)
ft_rates2
```


#### Plot of log event rates for each stratum
```{r plot of event rates log,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
#plot to confirm normality
ggplot(data=log_lambda_u)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=V1), fill="#FCCDE5")+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(I))+
  labs(title="Simulated log rates for strata 1-4")+ 
  xlab("")
```


### Hazard ratios for each stratum {.tabset}

For this mock-up we estimate hazard ratios for each time period (based roughly on published subgroup analysis). For the real analysis, the strata may be different; this is for demonstration only. We assume HR are independent; and make a guess at the HR for young men. We then split into two time periods: up to 18 months, where the HR are approximately 1; and beyond 18 months. We simulate 10,000 samples assuming a log normal distribution, for t1 and t2.


```{r mock hazard ratios - whole trial}
# overall HR. These come from the subgroup analysis of the trial
#create vector for each with HR, LCI, UCI
male_hr<- c(0.71,0.42,1.15)
female_hr<- c(0.44,0.21,0.94)
f_m_interaction <- female_hr/male_hr

under_60_hr<- c(0.76,0.44,1.33)
over_60_hr<- c(0.61,0.41,0.92)
old_young_interaction<- over_60_hr/under_60_hr

#assume independence; can multiply to get HR for subgroups
#need to make assumption on HR for young men

hrs_t2<- as.data.frame(rbind(
hr_young_men<- c(0.4, 0.26, 0.8),
hr_young_women <- hr_young_men*f_m_interaction,
hr_old_men<- hr_young_men*old_young_interaction,
hr_old_women <- hr_young_men*f_m_interaction*old_young_interaction
))
# we need to split into two time strata; the first 18 months and beyond 18 months. For the first 18 months, the HR between treatment and control overall is approximately 1; beyond this the HR widens. for the first 18 months, recentre HR and CI to 1ish. For beyond 18 months, use existing HR
hrs_t1<- as.data.frame(rbind(
hr_young_men_t1<- hr_young_men+0.42,
hr_young_women_t1 <- hr_young_women+0.62,
hr_old_men_t1 <- hr_old_men+0.58,
hr_old_women_t1 <- hr_old_women + 0.72
))

colnames(hrs_t1) <- c("HR", "l_ci", "u_ci")
rownames(hrs_t1) <- c(" Male <65 years", "Female <65 years", "Male 65 years and over", "Female 65 years and over")
colnames(hrs_t2) <- c("HR", "l_ci", "u_ci")
rownames(hrs_t2) <- c(" Male <65 years", "Female <65 years", "Male 65 years and over", "Female 65 years and over")
```

#### Hazard ratio for period $\tau_1$
```{r hr for t1}

ft_hr_t1<- flextable(round(hrs_t1,2))
ft_hr_t1<- set_header_labels(ft_hr_t1, HR="Hazard ratio", l_ci="Lower CI", u_ci="Upper CI")
ft_hr_t1_a <- width(ft_hr_t1,width=dim_pretty(ft_hr_t1)$widths)
ft_hr_t1_a

```

#### Hazard ratio for period $\tau_2$

```{r hr for t2}
ft_hr_t2<- flextable(round(hrs_t2,2))
ft_hr_t2<- set_header_labels(ft_hr_t2, HR="Hazard ratio", l_ci="Lower CI", u_ci="Upper CI")
ft_hr_t2_a <- width(ft_hr_t2,width=dim_pretty(ft_hr_t2)$widths)
ft_hr_t2_a
```

#### Histogram of simulated log hazard ratios for strata 1-4, $\tau_1$

```{r simulate n hrs, warnings=F,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
#obtain log of hrs and CI at t1 and t2. Assume log normally distributed, and find log standard error
setDT(hrs_t1)
setDT(hrs_t2)
hrs_t1[, log_hr:= log(HR)][, log_se:= (log(u_ci)-log(l_ci))/3.92][, se:= exp(log_se)]
hrs_t2[, log_hr:= log(HR)][, log_se:= (log(u_ci)-log(l_ci))/3.92][, se:= exp(log_se)]

#simulate log hr for nrep samples.
#create simulated hr for all strata
log_hrs_t1_per_strata <- hrs_t1[,rnorm(nrep, mean=log_hr, sd=log_se), by=.I]

#rename I column to the strata names
log_hrs_t1_per_strata$I <- factor(log_hrs_t1_per_strata$I, levels=c(1,2,3,4), labels=strata)


#plot to confirm normality
ggplot(data=log_hrs_t1_per_strata)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=V1), fill="#FCCDE5")+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(I))+
  labs(title="Simulated log hazard ratios for strata 1-4, T1")+ 
  xlab("Log of hazard ratio")
```

#### Histogram of simulated hazard ratios for strata 1-4, $\tau_2$

```{r hist hr t1 exponentiated,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
ggplot(data=log_hrs_t1_per_strata)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=exp(V1)), fill="#FCCDE5")+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(I))+
  labs(title="Simulated hazard ratios for strata 1-4, T1")+ 
  xlab("Hazard ratio")
```

#### Histogram of simulated log hazard ratios for strata 1-4, $\tau_2$

```{r t2 hr hists,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
#repeat for t2 hazard ratios
#create simulated hr for all strata
log_hrs_t2_per_strata <- hrs_t2[,rnorm(nrep, mean=log_hr, sd=log_se), by=.I]

#rename I column to the strata names
log_hrs_t2_per_strata$I <- factor(log_hrs_t2_per_strata$I, levels=c(1,2,3,4), labels=strata)

#plot to confirm normality
ggplot(data=log_hrs_t2_per_strata)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=V1), fill="#80B1D3")+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(I))+
  labs(title="Simulated log hazard ratios for strata 1-4, T2")+ 
  xlab("Log of hazard ratio")
```

#### Histogram of simulated hazard ratios for strata 1-4, $\tau_2$ 

```{r t2 hr exponentiated,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
ggplot(data=log_hrs_t2_per_strata)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=exp(V1)), fill="#80B1D3")+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(I))+
  labs(title="Simulated hazard ratios for strata 1-4, T2")+ 
  xlab("Hazard ratio")
```

### Calibrated rates and CIs for each stratum {.tabset}

For each age/sex stratum and for each time period, we combine the rate in the untreated and the hazard ratio to obtain a risk ratio.

Recall:

$$\lambda_u=\text{rate in untreated}$$


Multiply untreated rates by hazard ratios ($HR$) to get treated event rates in each time period:
$$\lambda_{treat1} = \lambda_u \times HR_1$$
$$\lambda_{treat2} = \lambda_u \times HR_2$$

We then transform these to risks for each stratum:
$$R_{untreated} = 1 - exp{(-\lambda_u(\tau_1+\tau_2)})$$ 

and 

$$R_{treated} = 1 - exp{(-(\lambda_{treat1} \tau_1 + \lambda_{treat2} \tau_2)})$$

The risk ratio at the end of the trial for each stratum is then:

$$ RR_S= \frac{R_{treated}}{R_{untreated}}$$

We have already simulated 10,000 samples for each hazard ratio and event rate, to generate 10,000 risk ratios.

```{r risks treated and untreated 2}
#set t1 and t2 in days
t1<-548
t2<- 4.5*365.25

#lambda from parametric model above
#lambda_u <- exp(z_lambda) 
log_lambda_u_sim<- log_lambda_u$V1

#for rate in treated, log lambda_t = log lambda_u + log hr
log_lambda_t1_sim <- log_lambda_u_sim+log_hrs_t1_per_strata$V1
log_lambda_t2_sim <- log_lambda_u_sim+log_hrs_t2_per_strata$V1

#exponentiate to get rates
lambda_u_sim <- exp(log_lambda_u_sim)
lambda_t1_sim<- exp(log_lambda_t1_sim)
lambda_t2_sim<- exp(log_lambda_t2_sim)

#transform to risks
risk_u1 <- 1-exp(-lambda_u_sim*t1)
risk_t1 <- 1-exp(-lambda_t1_sim*t1)
risk_u2 <- 1-exp(-lambda_u_sim*(t1+t2))
risk_t2 <- 1-exp(-(lambda_t1_sim*t1+lambda_t2_sim*t2))
risk_ratio_t1 <- risk_t1/risk_u1
risk_ratio_t2 <- risk_t2/risk_u2

risk_ratio_for_plot_t1 <- as.data.frame(cbind(c(rep(1,nrep), rep(2, nrep), rep(3, nrep), rep(4, nrep)), risk_ratio_t1))
risk_ratio_for_plot_t2 <- as.data.frame(cbind(c(rep(1,nrep), rep(2, nrep), rep(3, nrep), rep(4, nrep)), risk_ratio_t2))

rr_means_t1<- risk_ratio_for_plot_t1%>%
  group_by(V1)%>%
  summarize(means_t1=round(mean(risk_ratio_t1),3))

rr_means_t2<- risk_ratio_for_plot_t2%>%
  group_by(V1)%>%
  summarize(means_t2=round(mean(risk_ratio_t2),3))

```

#### Simulated risk ratios for strata 1-4, $t_1$ 

```{r risk plot t1,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
ggplot(data=risk_ratio_for_plot_t1)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=risk_ratio_t1), fill="#FCCDE5")+
  geom_text(data=rr_means_t1, aes(x=1.25, y=1000, label= paste("Mean=",means_t1)), size=3)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(V1))+
  labs(title=quote("Simulated risk ratios for strata 1-4, "* t[1]* "."))+ 
  xlab("Risk ratio")
```

#### Simulated risk ratios for strata 1-4, $t_2$

```{r risk plot t2,fig.width=6,fig.height=4}
ggplot(data=risk_ratio_for_plot_t2)+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=risk_ratio_t2), fill="#80B1D3")+
  geom_text(data=rr_means_t2, aes(x=1, y=1000, label= paste("Mean=",means_t2)), size=3)+
  theme_bw()+
  facet_wrap(vars(V1))+
  labs(title= quote("Simulated risk ratios for strata 1-4, "* t[2]* "."))+ 
  xlab("Risk ratio")

```

### Pooled risk ratio

The final step is to pool the risk ratio for each stratum. We take a weighted mean, where the risk ratio for each stratum is weighted by the number of people in each stratum. 

$$RR_{overall}= \sum_{s} RR_s n_s $$

We do this for all samples, then generate a mean and confidence intervals for the calibrated relative risk.



```{r pooling hrs}
#matrix of risk ratios at t1, one column per stratum

rr_t1<-matrix(risk_ratio_t1, ncol=4)
rr_t2<-matrix(risk_ratio_t2, ncol=4)

#matrix of ns in latch, one column per stratum
strata_n_df<- as.data.frame(t(strata_n))
ns <- as.matrix(strata_n_df %>% slice(rep(1, each = nrep)))
#create a vector of totals
n_tot<- rowSums(ns)

## calculate n x rr_mtrx (note that this is NOT matrix multiplication)
#t1
rn_t1 <- rowSums(rr_t1*ns)
rr_tot_t1 <- rn_t1/n_tot
cis_t1<- round(quantile(rr_tot_t1, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)),3)
est_t1<- round(mean(rr_tot_t1),3)

#t2
rn_t2 <- rowSums(rr_t2*ns)
rr_tot_t2 <- rn_t2/n_tot
cis_t2<- round(quantile(rr_tot_t2, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)),3)
est_t2<- round(mean(rr_tot_t2),3)
```



#### Calibrated risk ratio at the end of the trial, for the whole trial

```{r t2 pooled results numerical, comment=NA}

cat(paste("  The estimated calibrated risk ratio is ", est_t2, " (95% confidence interval ", cis_t2[1], "-", cis_t2[2], ").", sep=""))

```

\n
\n
```{r t2 pooled results,results="asis",fig.width=4,fig.height=3}

ggplot(data=as.data.frame(rr_tot_t2))+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=rr_tot_t2), fill="#80B1D3")+
  theme_bw()+
  labs(title=quote("Simulated risk ratio for the whole trial at "* t[2]* ".) "))+ 
  xlab("Risk ratio")


```

### Absolute risk 

We can use the new, calibrated risk ratio to estimate the number needed to treat (NNT) in the LATCH cohort from the absolute risk ratio (ARR). We use 10,000 samples of the risk ratio, and generate confidence intervals.

$$ARR = 1/(\lambda_u - \lambda_u \times RR_{overall})$$

$$ \text{NNT} = 1/\text{ARR} $$

```{r absolute risks, comment=NA}
#without simulation
event_rate<- sum(strata_n_events_t2)/sum(strata_fu_t2)*365.25
arr <- event_rate- event_rate*rr_tot_t2
nnt <- 1/arr
cis_nnt<- quantile(nnt, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))
cis_arr<- round(quantile(arr, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)),4)

#with simulation
# event_rate_sim<- lambda_u_sim*365.25
# arr_sim <- (event_rate_sim- event_rate_sim*rr_tot_t2)
# nnt_sim<- 1/arr_sim
# cis_nnt_sim<- quantile(nnt_sim, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))
# cis_arr_sim<- round(quantile(arr_sim, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)),4)

cat(paste(" The number needed to treat is ", round(mean(nnt),2)," (95% CI ", round(cis_nnt[1],2), " - ", round(cis_nnt[2],2),").", sep=""))
# cat(paste(" Using simulation and our exponential model, the number needed to treat is ",round(mean(nnt_sim),2)," (95% CI ", round(cis_nnt_sim[1],2), " - ", round(cis_nnt_sim[2],2),").", sep=""))


```

## Extension to adverse events

Benefits of treatment must be balanced against potential risks, and so it might be informative to calibrate adverse events. In TRIDENT, adverse events of special interest were hypotension, syncope, headache, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, injurious falls,and acute kidney injury. There was also a protocol requirement of discontinuation if serum creatinine increased level of 20% or more from the baseline value (creatinine was measured at baseline, 6 weeks and at the end of the trial). 

Most of these adverse events were not measured in LATCH, but we have applied to link the LATCH cohort to routinely-collected data in Lothian, including renal function measurements. While a decline in renal function might not lead to discontinuation of a drug, it might have important implications for drug monitoring.

If sufficient people in LATCH have repeat measurements of renal function, we could apply a similar method to above, to calibrate the rate of decline in renal function to a specified threshold (eg >20% increase in creatinine, >30% decline in eGFR). 

## References

<div id="refs"></div>


## Appendix

We can pool the risk ratio at $t_1$, to find the calibrated relative risk after 18 months.

```{r t1 pooled results printout, comment=NA}
cat(paste("The estimated calibrated risk ratio is ", est_t1, " (95% confidence interval ", cis_t1[1], "-", cis_t1[2], ")", sep=""))
```

\n
\n

```{r t1 pooled results,results="asis",fig.width=3,fig.height=2}
ggplot(data=as.data.frame(rr_tot_t1))+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=rr_tot_t1), fill="#FCCDE5")+
  theme_bw()+
  labs(title="Simulated risk ratio, T1")+ 
  xlab("Risk ratio")

```
